---
bibliography: refs.bib
---
## Background
Cetaceans are widely regarded as indicator species for marine ecosystem health. As upper-trophic-level predators they integrate ecological pressures across multiple trophic levels and broad spatial scales, such that their distribution and abundance can serve as a reliable proxy for the condition of the ecosystems they inhabit [@cite]. Robust, spatially explicit knowledge of cetacean occurrence is therefore a prerequisite for effective marine spatial planning and for the design and evaluation of conservation measures.

The Strait of Gibraltar is among the most ecologically significant and heavily trafficked marine corridors globally. As the sole connection between the Atlantic Ocean and the Mediterranean Sea, it supports a diverse cetacean community [@destephanisSummerSpatialDistribution2008; @mannocciAssessingCetaceanSurveys2018; @canales-caceresDistributionBehaviourStriped2023; @gnoneCetaceansMediterraneanSea2023; @olaya-ponzoneSpatioTemporalPatternsLimited2026] and its importance is formally recognised through designation as an `#gls("imma")`{=typst} by the IUCN Marine Mammal Protected Areas Task Force [@SummaryCurrentIMMAs], connected to the adjacent Atlantic Coast of the Iberian Peninsula `#gls("imma")`{=typst}. The regional assemblage is dominated by delphinids—short-beaked common dolphin (*Delphinus delphis*), striped dolphin (*Stenella coeruleoalba*), and common bottlenose dolphin (*Tursiops truncatus*), alongside seasonal occurrence of larger cetaceans including fin whales (*Balaenoptera physalus*) migrating between the Atlantic and Mediterranean [@canales-caceresDistributionBehaviourStriped2023; @gnoneCetaceansMediterraneanSea2023; @olaya-ponzoneMixedspeciesDolphinGroups2025; @olaya-ponzoneSpatioTemporalPatternsLimited2026]. Three of these species were recorded during the present study (@fig-species).

![The three cetacean species recorded during the present study: short-beaked common dolphin (*Delphinus delphis*) (A), striped dolphin (*Stenella coeruleoalba*) (B), and fin whales (*Balaenoptera physalus*) (C).](/misc/intro_dolph.png){#fig-species}

Despite this ecological significance, cetacean occurrence within the `#gls("bgtw")`{=typst} has not been documented by a dedicated systematic survey. Existing records derive exclusively from opportunistic observation and ferry-based monitoring—approaches that, while valuable for establishing species presence, are subject to substantial spatial and temporal bias and cannot yield reliable estimates of density or distribution [@bucklandIntroductionDistanceSampling2001; @dawsonDesignFieldMethods2008; @bucklandDistanceSamplingMethods2015; @gnoneCetaceansMediterraneanSea2023]. This represents a clear gap in the understanding of marine biodiversity within a bounded, heavily utilised territory of high conservation relevance.

Distance sampling offers a rigorous, effort-corrected framework for addressing this gap, allowing density and distribution to be estimated from systematically collected sighting data rather than inferred from presence records alone [@bucklandIntroductionDistanceSampling2001; @bucklandDistanceSamplingMethods2015]. The present study applies this approach to provide the first systematic assessment of cetacean spatial distribution and relative abundance within the `#gls("bgtw")`{=typst}, and to evaluate systematic survey against the existing opportunistic record. Specifically, the study aimed to: (1) characterise cetacean species spatial distribution and relative density within the BGTW; (2) compare systematic survey outputs against existing opportunistic records to evaluate the relative value of each monitoring approach; (3) collect opportunistic photo-identification data to support species assessment; and (4) provide an evidence base to inform conservation and management within this bounded territory.


## Literature Review
The eastern Strait of Gibraltar and the adjacent Alboran Sea constitute a transitional zone between the Atlantic and the Mediterranean and support a diverse odontocete assemblage, including the three Delphinidae that were the focus of this study: bottlenose dolphin , short-beaked common dolphin and striped dolphin [@canadasModelbasedAbundanceEstimates2023; @destephanisSummerSpatialDistribution2008; @olaya-ponzoneSpatioTemporalPatternsLimited2026; @olaya-ponzonePhotoIdentificationCatalogueDelphinus2025]. Larger cetaceans, including fin whales, were recorded when encountered but were not included in the distance-sampling analysis. Regional cetacean occurrence in this sector has been characterised largely through habitat-based distribution modelling and photo-identification of these species [@canadasModelbasedAbundanceEstimates2023; @olaya-ponzonePhotoIdentificationCatalogueDelphinus2025; @olaya-ponzoneSpatioTemporalPatternsLimited2026; @bamfordAnalysisDorsalEdge2016], and the wider basin has been assessed through large-scale aerial and shipboard line-transect programmes, most recently the `#gls("accobams")`{=typst} Survey Initiative [@panigadaACCOBAMSSurveyInitiative2024]. Despite this regional coverage, no dedicated systematic survey has been conducted within the `#gls("bgtw")`{=typst}: existing local datasets are predominantly opportunistic, behavioural or photo-identification based [@olaya-ponzonePhotoIdentificationCatalogueDelphinus2025; @bamfordAnalysisDorsalEdge2016; @olaya-ponzoneMixedspeciesDolphinGroups2025] rather than design-based abundance and distribution estimates.

This literature review was undertaken to position the present study within that methodological and geographic landscape. Its aims were (i) to characterise the methods currently applied to cetacean distribution research, with an emphasis on systematic survey design; (ii) to quantify the geographic and taxonomic distribution of that research effort across the Mediterranean; and (iii) to identify the gap that a design-based survey of the `#gls("bgtw")`{=typst} is intended to address.


### Critical Review
A systematic review was conducted to provide an overview of the current research that has been conducted on cetacean distribution using systematic survey design, with a particular focus on the Western Mediterranean area. The following key word strings were utilised in order to scan the Google Scholar database:

```{=typst}
#text(style: "italic")[Distance sampling AND cetacean; cetacean\* AND "western Mediterranean"; Dolphin AND "western mediterranean" AND (coastal usage / habitat use); kernel density estimation AND cetacean] 
\ 
\ 
```

The search returned 51,180 records. Because Google Scholar returns results in relevance-ranked order and does not permit bulk export, the first 2,342 records were screened against the relevance matrix (@tbl-relevance, @tbl-incl); following the PRISMA framework (@fig-prisma), 104 publications were retained. Reliance on a single database is a recognised limitation of this approach, as Google Scholar results are not fully reproducible and vary with search context; the review should therefore be read as indicative of the research landscape rather than an exhaustive enumeration.

Records were screened by method and species and scored using the relevance matrix, with publications scoring three or above retained for full screening. Further publications were identified by searching the reference lists of retained papers (snowballing). Retained publications were then screened for the survey method, focal species, and geographic extent.

![The PRISMA framework was used to systematically decide if publications fit the criteria and should be included in the final dissertation.](figures/litreview/prisma.png){#fig-prisma}

A screening of records was conducted to identify methods and species, and these were then scored using the relevance matrix (@tbl-relevance, @tbl-incl). Papers with scores of three or more were retained for further screening. A further series of publications were obtained through a comprehensive search of the reference lists of returned papers. The final papers were screened specifically for the method used, the species, and the area extent.

| Score | Method | Species | Study Area | State of the Art | Outcome |
|:-----:|:------:|:-------:|:----------:|:----------------:|:--------|
| 1 | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | Excluded |
| 2 | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | Excluded |
| 3 | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | Retained |
| 4 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | Retained |
| 5 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Retained – fits all criteria |

: Relevance scoring matrix used to screen publications. Criteria are applied cumulatively, such that a higher score requires all lower-ranked criteria to also be met. Publications scoring three or above were retained for further screening. {#tbl-relevance tbl-colwidths="[8,12,12,14,20,34]"}

| Criterion | Description | 
|:-------|:------|
| **Method** | Distance Sampling; Line transect; (spatial) distribution models; land-based survey |
| **Species** | Dolphins, striped dolphin (*Stenella coeruleoalba*), bottlenose dolphin (*Tursiops truncatus*), short-beaked common dolphin (*Delphinus delphis*) |
| **Study Area** | Western Mediterranean, Bay of Algeciras / Gibraltar, Strait of Gibraltar |
| **State of the Art** | Relevant; reviewed and up to date, or addresses known biases |

: Inclusion criteria applied at each level of the relevance matrix. {#tbl-incl tbl-colwidths="[20,80]"}


### Synthesis Review
**Geographic distribution of effort.** Among the 58 records assignable to a Mediterranean subregion, the Western Mediterranean was most frequently represented (n = 22), ahead of Mediterranean-wide studies (n = 14) and the Northwestern Mediterranean (n = 13), with comparatively little work in the eastern basin (Eastern and Northeastern Mediterranean, n = 5 and n = 4, respectively; @fig-lit_map). This concentration partly reflects the search terms, but is consistent with a genuine regional research focus driven by the Alboran Sea's recognised importance for the focal species [@canadasModelbasedAbundanceEstimates2023; @arcangeliTestingIndicatorsTrend2023; @panigadaACCOBAMSSurveyInitiative2024]. This effort is dominated by regional- and basin-scale designs; fine-scale, design-based coverage of the Bay of Gibraltar and `#gls("bgtw")`{=typst} sector is largely absent, reinforcing the gap this study addresses.

![The overview map provides a synoptic representation of the studies and the countries in which they were conducted. The focus map (inset) delineates the Mediterranean region into distinct subregions. WM: Western Mediterranean; NWM: Northwestern Mediterranean; NEM: Northeastern Mediterranean; EM: Eastern Mediterranean; M: Mediterranean not specified.](figures/litreview/world_combined.png){#fig-lit_map}

**Taxonomic and methodological focus.** Research effort was concentrated on bottlenose dolphin (*Tursiops truncatus*), the most frequently studied single species (n = 21), followed by short-beaked common dolphin (*Delphinus delphis*, n = 10) and striped dolphin (*Stenella coeruleoalba*, n = 6); striped and common dolphin also featured prominently within multi-species assemblage studies (@fig-lit_heat). Methodologically, line-transect distance sampling was the most common single approach, followed by photo-identification mark–recapture and spatial distribution modelling, with aerial line-transect surveys frequently combined with distance sampling. Land-based survey, by contrast, was applied in only a small number of studies. The combination of this methodological under-use with the absence of fine-scale coverage of the `#gls("bgtw")`{=typst} defines a two-fold gap—of method and of place—that the present study addresses.

![Species-by-method matrix quantifying the number of publications applying each survey method to each focal species. Species and methods were extracted from the papers compiled in the critical review.](figures/litreview/heatmap.png){#fig-lit_heat}

**Temporal trend.** Publication output increased over the review period, from few studies before 2000 to a marked rise across 2015–2024, reflecting the growth of coordinated monitoring programmes and improvements in survey and analytical methods over this interval (@fig-time).

![Number of retained publications by year of publication.](figures/litreview/histogram.png){#fig-time}
